content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
by SB14

August 29, 1990

Robert E. Leideigh
Olson, Connelly, Hagel, Fong & Leideigh
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-90-492

Dear Mr. Leideigh:

     This is in response to your letter requesting assistance
concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").1/ Your letter was initially submitted to the Commission as comments on the Commission memorandum concerning political
endorsements which was heard at the August 7, 1990 Commission
meeting. As we discussed on July 26, 1990, the memorandum
requested by the Commission did not concern the issues to which
your letter pertains.

     Also, your request does not concern a specific governmental
decision or named public officials with responsibilities under the
Act, so we believe it appropriate to treat your request as one for
informal assistance.2/ Further, since the analysis contained
herein is necessarily a factual analysis, responses may differ
depending on the actual facts involved.

PRELIMINARY DISCUSSION

     The passage of Proposition 73 in June of 1988 imposed a
series of new restrictions on the use of campaign funds. Section
85201(c) requires candidates for public office to deposit all
campaign contributions they receive into a campaign bank account.
Further, Section 85202(b) requires that a candidate hold his or

_____________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-492
Page 2


her campaign contributions in trust and only expend them for
certain purposes. Specifically, Section 85202(b) states:

     All contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office for which the
candidate has stated, pursuant to Section 85200,
that he or she intends to seek or expenses associ-
ated with holding that office.

     Effective January 1, 1990, the Legislature added Section
85801 to the Act. Section 85801 states in pertinent part:

     An expenditure to seek office is within the
lawful execution of the trust imposed by Section
85202 if it is reasonably related to a political
purpose. An expenditure associated with holding
office is within the lawful execution of the trust
imposed by Section 85202 if it is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose.
Expenditures which confer a substantial personal
benefit shall not be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose.

     The Commission, both before and after the effective date of
Section 85801, has interpreted Section 85202(b) to require a clear
relationship between a candidate's campaign expenditure and his or
her campaign for office in order for the expenditure to be
legitimately associated with seeking that office. In addition,
since the effective date of Section 85801, such an expenditure is
not permitted if it otherwise violates the prohibitions set forth
in Sections 85802, 85802.5, 85804 or 85805.3/ (Milman Advice
Letter, No. I-90-206, copy enclosed.)

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

     1. A local elected official has three more years to run in
her current term. There is a statewide ballot measure on the is-
sue of abortion. The local official wishes to contribute from
campaign funds to the ballot measure in order to demonstrate her
support for the issue. She knows many of her constituents feel
the abortion issue is very important. However, she had no opposi-
tion in her last reelection campaign and likely will have no op-
position in her next campaign. Consequently, it is impossible to
tell at this time whether the abortion question will be an issue
in her next campaign three years hence. May she contribute with
campaign funds?

_________________________
3/ Your letter assumes compliance with the personal use
statutes. Thus, the analysis in the advice letter focuses solely
on the trust provision of the Act.

Our File No. I-90-492
Page 3


The candidate may make such a contribution if she has made
the ballot measure an issue in her own campaign for election.
Under our advice in the Milman Advice Letter (supra), an
expenditure of campaign funds does not violate the trust provision
of Section 85202(b) if it is clearly related to the candidate's
election to office. Making an expenditure on a ballot measure
which concerns an issue in the candidate's campaign and which will
have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the candidate's
constituents is clearly related to the candidate's election to
office.

2. A local elected official has three more years to run on
his current term. Rent control is an important issue in his community. In another local jurisdiction (100 miles away) there is a
ballot measure dealing with rent control. He wishes to contribute
to the ballot measure dealing with rent control. He wishes to
contribute to the ballot measure in order to show his support for
the issue. There is a case currently pending before the United
States Supreme Court which may resolve all rent control issues on
constitutional grounds. The case will be decided after the ballot
measure is voted on, but before the local elected official's next
campaign. Depending on the result of the Supreme Court decision,
the rent control issue may be moot long before the local elected
official's next reelection campaign. May he contribute to the
ballot measure with his campaign funds?

The candidate may not make the contribution. Although the
ballot measure may concern an issue in the candidate's campaign, a
ballot measure in a jurisdiction 100 miles away does not appear to
have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the candidate's
constituents. Based on these facts, the expenditure is not
clearly related to the candidate's election to office.

3. A local elected official believes strongly that smoking
and its side effects (including "second-hand smoke) are
detrimental to human health. She has been in the forefront of
efforts at the local level to adopt and implement no smoking
ordinances governing public buildings of her local jurisdiction.
She is in the first year of a four-year term. Tobacco industry
lobbyists are actively lobbying the state legislature to adopt
statewide standards for regulating smoking in public (and private
buildings). The anti-smoking forces are considering circulating a
statewide initiative to counter such legislation. They have
solicited her for a contribution. In her recent election
campaign, growth control, gang violence and a sports stadium were
the major campaign issues. The smoking ordinance was not.
However, there is strong public support in her community for the
ordinance. May she contribute to the statewide ballot measure
campaign from her campaign funds?

Our File No. I-90-492
Page 4


     For the reasons set forth in our answer to Question 1 above,
and subject to the conditions set forth, the candidate may make
such a contribution.

     4. A state legislator represents a district which includes a
jurisdiction with a strong rent control ordinance. His next elec-
tion will be in two years. Another jurisdiction which has a
similar strong rent control ordinance has a ballot measure to be
voted on this year which would significantly weaken that
jurisdiction's rent control ordinance. Tenant activists in the
legislator's district fear that if the ballot measure succeeds, it
will increase the likelihood of a similar measure being proposed
in their jurisdiction. They urge the legislator to contribute to
the campaign against the ballot measure. May he do so with his
campaign funds?

     Absent additional facts, we rely on the rationale set forth
in our answer to Question 2 above. Therefore, the candidate may
not make the contribution.

     5. Suppose instead of a ballot measure, there is a lawsuit
which has been brought challenging the constitutionality of the
rent control measure. The defenders of the other jurisdiction's
rent control measure are raising funds to provide for the legal
defense of the measure. May the legislator contribute to the
legal defense fund from campaign funds? Would the answer be dif-
ferent in either of the above situations if the ballot measure or
lawsuit involved the rent control ordinance in the legislator's
own district?

     The candidate may make such a contribution if the subject
matter of the lawsuit is an issue in the candidate's campaign and
the lawsuit will have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the
candidate's constituents. This latter requirement would be met if
the lawsuit was in the candidate's own jurisdiction. A Superior
Court judgment concerning an ordinance in another jurisdiction may
not have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the candidate's
constituents. Conversely, an Appellate Court decision
invalidating an identical ordinance in another jurisdiction would
affect the candidate's constituents.

     6. A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser of a
local candidate for federal office. The purpose of attending is
to demonstrate solidarity with the federal candidate (and thereby
acquire some party identification for a "nonpartisan" office).
May the local candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to
the fundraiser?

     Generally contributions to or expenditures on federal
candidates are not associated with a local candidate's election to
office. However, where there is a clear benefit to the local
candidate's campaign, expenditures which may also benefit a

Our File No. I-90-492
Page 5


federal candidate are permissible. In this case, the districts of
both the local candidate and the federal candidate overlap and the
local candidate's participation in the federal candidate's
fundraiser could foreseeably result in partisan support for the
local candidate by supporters of the federal candidate.
Therefore, this expenditure is permitted.47

7. A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser of a
candidate for federal office from a different locale. The purpose
of attending is to mingle with contributors to the federal
candidate who might be induced to contribute to the local
candidate, since the two share similar views on many issues. May
the local candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the
fundraiser?

As stated above, normally, a local candidate's mere
contribution of campaign funds to a federal candidate does not
appear to be an expenditure that is clearly related to his
election to office. However, under some circumstances an
expenditure to attend a political event, even an event on behalf
of a federal candidate, may be permissible. Under the facts
presented, even though the federal candidate does not represent
the local candidate's district, the local candidate seeks to use
his attendance at the event to generate contributions to his own
campaign. This may be a permissible use of campaign funds if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the local candidate's attendance at
the local fundraiser will generate these contributions. However,
absent additional facts we cannot make this determination.

8. A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser of a
local candidate for federal office. The purpose of attending is
to show that the local office candidate has the ability to "get
the ear" of the candidate for federal office. This would
demonstrate the local candidate's ability to seek federal as-
sistance for issues of local concern. May the local candidate use
campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?

The answer will be the same as the answer to Question 6
above.

9. A legislative candidate wishes to attend a fundraiser for
her party's candidate for President. May she use campaign funds
to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?


_______________________
47/ Section 85304 of the Act prohibits the transfer of campaign
contributions to other candidates. Thus, despite justification of
the expenditure under the trust provision, a California candidate
may not spend campaign funds to attend another California
candidate's fundraiser.

Our File No. I-90-492
Page 6


     Absent more information, the answer would be the same as the
answer to Question 6 above in the sense that the presidential
candidate is also a candidate in the local candidate's own
jurisdiction and her participation in the the presidential
candidate's fundraiser will foreseeably generate support for her
own candidacy, as well as media exposure and potential
contributions.

     10. A local candidate has two years to run on her term. She
may or may not decide to seek reelection; however, she has filed a
Form 501 for reelection. She, and many of her constituents, are
committed on the abortion issue. A ballot measure is pending in
Arizona on the abortion question. It is expected to make the test
case which the United States Supreme Court will use to determine
the continuing applicability of Roe v. Wade. May she use campaign
funds to contribute to the ballot measure committee in Arizona?

     Absent additional facts, we rely on the rationale set forth
in our answer to Question 2 above. Therefore, the candidate may
not make the contribution.

     11. A local candidate is running for election in a district
whose voters are predominantly Black. Andrew Young is in a runoff
for the nomination for Governor of Georgia. May the local
candidate use campaign funds to contribute to Andrew Young?

     The candidate may not make the expenditure. An outright gift
of campaign funds to another candidate is not clearly associated
with the California candidate's election to office even if the
election is an issue in the California candidate's campaign. The
election of a candidate in another jurisdiction does not affect
the California candidate's jurisdiction.

     12. Suppose the candidate in question eleven is a candidate
for state legislative office in a district whose voters are
predominantly Black. Would the answer be any different?

     The answer is the same as in Question 11.

     13. Suppose the candidate in Question 11 is running in a
district with no significant Black population. Would the answer
be any different?

     The answer is the same as in Question 11.

Our File No. I-90-492
Page 7


     14.  A candidate for local office is a woman.  She is receiv-
ing campaign funding support from the National Women's Political
Caucus.  She has been asked to, in turn, supply campaign funding
support for other women candidates in other states.  May she use
her campaign funds for this purpose?

     Absent additional facts, the rationale in Question 11 above
will apply.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:dg

Enclosures

sections.question:
S AND CONCLUSIONS

1. A local elected official has three more years to run in her current term. There is a statewide ballot measure on the issue of abortion. The local official wishes to contribute from campaign funds to the ballot measure in order to demonstrate her support for the issue. She knows many of her constituents feel the abortion issue is very important. However, she had no opposition in her last reelection campaign and likely will have no opposition in her next campaign. Consequently, it is impossible to tell at this time whether the abortion question will be an issue in her next campaign three years hence. May she contribute with campaign funds?

3/ Your letter assumes compliance with the personal use statutes. Thus, the analysis in the advice letter focuses solely on the trust provision of the Act.

The candidate may make such a contribution if she has made the ballot measure an issue in her own campaign for election. Under our advice in the Milman Advice Letter (supra), an expenditure of campaign funds does not violate the trust provision of Section 85202(b) if it is clearly related to the candidate's election to office. Making an expenditure on a ballot measure which concerns an issue in the candidate's campaign and which will have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the candidate's constituents is clearly related to the candidate's election to office.

2. A local elected official has three more years to run on his current term. Rent control is an important issue in his community. In another local jurisdiction (100 miles away) there is a ballot measure dealing with rent control. He wishes to contribute to the ballot measure dealing with rent control. He wishes to contribute to the ballot measure in order to show his support for the issue. There is a case currently pending before the United States Supreme Court which may resolve all rent control issues on constitutional grounds. The case will be decided after the ballot measure is voted on, but before the local elected official's next campaign. Depending on the result of the Supreme Court decision, the rent control issue may be moot long before the local elected official's next reelection campaign. May he contribute to the ballot measure with his campaign funds?

The candidate may not make the contribution. Although the ballot measure may concern an issue in the candidate's campaign, a ballot measure in a jurisdiction 100 miles away does not appear to have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the candidate's constituents. Based on these facts, the expenditure is not clearly related to the candidate's election to office.

3. A local elected official believes strongly that smoking and its side effects (including "second-hand" smoke) are detrimental to human health. She has been in the forefront of efforts at the local level to adopt and implement no smoking ordinances governing public buildings of her local jurisdiction. Tobacco industry lobbyists are actively lobbying the state legislature to adopt statewide standards for regulating smoking in public (and private buildings). The anti-smoking forces are considering circulating a statewide initiative to counter such legislation. They have solicited her for a contribution. In her recent election campaign, growth control, gang violence and a sports stadium were the major campaign issues. The smoking ordinance was not. However, there is strong public support in her community for the ordinance. May she contribute to the statewide ballot measure campaign from her campaign funds?

For the reasons set forth in our answer to Question 1 above, and subject to the conditions set forth, the candidate may make such a contribution.

4. A state legislator represents a district which includes a jurisdiction with a strong rent control ordinance. His next election will be in two years. Another jurisdiction which has a similar strong rent control ordinance has a ballot measure to be voted on this year which would significantly weaken that jurisdiction's rent control ordinance. Tenant activists in the legislator's district fear that if the ballot measure succeeds, it will increase the likelihood of a similar measure being proposed in their jurisdiction. They urge the legislator to contribute to the campaign against the ballot measure. May he do so with his campaign funds?

Absent additional facts, we rely on the rationale set forth in our answer to Question 2 above. Therefore, the candidate may not make the contribution.

5. Suppose instead of a ballot measure, there is a lawsuit which has been brought challenging the constitutionality of the rent control measure. The defenders of the other jurisdiction's rent control measure are raising funds to provide for the legal defense of the measure. May the legislator contribute to the legal defense fund from campaign funds? Would the answer be different in either of the above situations if the ballot measure or lawsuit involved the rent control ordinance in the legislator's own district?

The candidate may make such a contribution if the subject matter of the lawsuit is an issue in the candidate's campaign and the lawsuit will have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the candidate's constituents. This latter requirement would be met if the lawsuit was in the candidate's own jurisdiction. A Superior Court judgment concerning an ordinance in another jurisdiction may not have a reasonably foreseeable impact on the candidate's constituents. Conversely, an Appellate Court decision invalidating an identical ordinance in another jurisdiction would affect the candidate's constituents.

6. A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser of a local candidate for federal office. The purpose of attending is to demonstrate solidarity with the federal candidate (and thereby acquire some party identification for a "nonpartisan" office). May the local candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?

Generally contributions to or expenditures on federal candidates are not associated with a local candidate's election to office. However, where there is a clear benefit to the local candidate's campaign, expenditures which may also benefit a

federal candidate are permissible. In this case, the districts of both the local candidate and the federal candidate overlap and the local candidate’s participation in the federal candidate’s fundraiser could foreseeably result in partisan support for the local candidate by supporters of the federal candidate. Therefore, this expenditure is permitted.4/

7. A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser of a candidate for federal office from a different locale. The purpose of attending is to mingle with contributors to the federal candidate who might be induced to contribute to the local candidate, since the two share similar views on many issues. May the local candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?

As stated above, normally, a local candidate’s mere contribution of campaign funds to a federal candidate does not appear to be an expenditure that is clearly related to his election to office. However, under some circumstances an expenditure to attend a political event, even an event on behalf of a federal candidate, may be permissible. Under the facts presented, even though the federal candidate does not represent the local candidate’s district, the local candidate seeks to use his attendance at the event to generate contributions to his own campaign. This may be a permissible use of campaign funds if it is reasonably foreseeable that the local candidate’s attendance at the local fundraiser will generate these contributions. However, absent additional facts we cannot make this determination.

8. A local candidate wishes to attend the fundraiser of a local candidate for federal office. The purpose of attending is to show that the local office candidate has the ability to "get the ear" of the candidate for federal office. This would demonstrate the local candidate’s ability to seek federal assistance for issues of local concern. May the local candidate use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?

The answer will be the same as the answer to Question 6 above.

9. A legislative candidate wishes to attend a fundraiser for her party’s candidate for President. May she use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to the fundraiser?

4/ Section 85304 of the Act prohibits the transfer of campaign contributions to other candidates. Thus, despite justification of the expenditure under the trust provision, a California candidate may not spend campaign funds to attend another California candidate’s fundraiser.

Absent more information, the answer would be the same as the answer to Question 6 above in the sense that the presidential candidate is also a candidate in the local candidate's own jurisdiction and her participation in the presidential candidate's fundraiser will foreseeably generate support for her own candidacy, as well as media exposure and potential contributions.

10. A local candidate has two years to run on her term. She may or may not decide to seek reelection; however, she has filed a Form 501 for reelection. She, and many of her constituents, are committed on the abortion issue. A ballot measure is pending in Arizona on the abortion question. It is expected to make the test case which the United States Supreme Court will use to determine the continuing applicability of Roe v. Wade. May she use campaign funds to contribute to the ballot measure committee in Arizona?

Absent additional facts, we rely on the rationale set forth in our answer to Question 2 above. Therefore, the candidate may not make the contribution.

11. A local candidate is running for election in a district whose voters are predominantly Black. Andrew Young is in a runoff for the nomination for Governor of Georgia. May the local candidate use campaign funds to contribute to Andrew Young?

The candidate may not make the expenditure. An outright gift of campaign funds to another candidate is not clearly associated with the California candidate's election to office even if the election is an issue in the California candidate's campaign. The election of a candidate in another jurisdiction does not affect the California candidate's jurisdiction.

12. Suppose the candidate in question eleven is a candidate for state legislative office in a district whose voters are predominately Black. Would the answer be any different?

The answer is the same as in Question 11.

13. Suppose the candidate in Question 11 is running in a district with no significant Black population. Would the answer be any different?

The answer is the same as in Question 11.

14. A candidate for local office is a woman. She is receiving campaign funding support from the National Women's Political Caucus. She has been asked to, in turn, supply campaign funding support for other women candidates in other states. May she use her campaign funds for this purpose?

Absent additional facts, the rationale in Question 11 above will apply.

Absent additional facts, the rationale in Question 11 above will apply.
embedding.first_500_words: **California Fair Political Practices Commission** **August 29, 1990** Robert E. Leidigh Olson, Connelly, Hagel, Fong & Leidigh 300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-90-492 Dear Mr. Leidigh: This is in response to your letter requesting assistance concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Your letter was initially submitted to the Commission as comments on the Commission memorandum concerning political endorsements which was heard at the August 7, 1990 Commission meeting. As we discussed on July 26, 1990, the memorandum requested by the Commission did not concern the issues to which your letter pertains. Also, your request does not concern a specific governmental decision or named public officials with responsibilities under the Act, so we believe it appropriate to treat your request as one for informal assistance.2/ Further, since the analysis contained herein is necessarily a factual analysis, responses may differ depending on the actual facts involved. PRELIMINARY DISCUSSION The passage of Proposition 73 in June of 1988 imposed a series of new restrictions on the use of campaign funds. Section 85201(c) requires candidates for public office to deposit all campaign contributions they receive into a campaign bank account. Further, Section 85202(b) requires that a candidate hold his or 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) her campaign contributions in trust and only expend them for certain purposes. Specifically, Section 85202(b) states: All contributions deposited into the campaign account shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate to the specific office for which the candidate has stated, pursuant to Section 85200, that he or she intends to seek or expenses associated with holding that office. Effective January 1, 1990, the Legislature added Section 85801 to the Act. Section 85801 states in pertinent part: An expenditure to seek office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by Section 85202 if it is reasonably related to a political purpose. An expenditure associated with holding office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by Section 85202 if it is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. Expenditures which confer a substantial personal benefit shall be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. The Commission, both before and after the effective date of Section 85801, has interpreted Section 85202(b) to require a clear relationship between a candidate's campaign expenditure and his or her campaign for office in order for the expenditure to be legitimately associated with seeking that office. In addition, since the effective date of Section 85801, such an expenditure is not
